title: The first time I cooked a foreign dish
summary: Tell the story of the first time you tried to cook a dish from a country you had never visited.
topic: The first time I cooked a dish from another country
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences about the first time you cooked a dish from a country that was not your own. Describe what happened, what went wrong or right, and how it tasted. Use past simple.
hintStarters: I decided to try making… because I had eaten it once at a restaurant and…
The hardest part was finding the right ingredient, so I…
When I tasted the first spoonful, I realised that…
It was not exactly right, but I felt…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự cố gắng, nỗ lực thử

sampleText: Two years ago, I decided to try making moussaka for the first time. I had eaten it once at a small Greek restaurant in my city and I wanted to make it at home. I found a recipe online, but when I looked at the ingredients, I realised I could not find one of them at the supermarket nearby. The recipe used a specific type of cheese that nobody had heard of in my country, so I bought a similar local one instead and hoped it would work. The hardest part was making the sauce, which needed to be thick but kept going too thin. I added more flour twice. When I finally put everything in the oven, I waited for an hour. The smell was good and I felt hopeful. When I tasted the first spoonful, I thought it was quite different from the restaurant version. The aubergines were a little undercooked and the sauce was too floury. But it was also warm and filling and I ate a large portion. I sent a photo to my sister and she replied, 'Looks nothing like moussaka. Are those raw potatoes?' I laughed. The second time I made it, three weeks later, it was much better.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một loại phô mai cụ thể

sampleAnnotations.phrase: bought a similar local one instead
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: mua một loại địa phương tương tự thay thế

sampleAnnotations.phrase: needed to be thick but kept going too thin
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: cần phải đặc nhưng cứ bị loãng
tôi thêm bột mì thêm hai lần nữa

sampleAnnotations.phrase: quite different from the restaurant version
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: khá khác với phiên bản ở nhà hàng
quá nhiều bột, nhiều bột quá
Trông không giống moussaka chút nào

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past simple with 'keep + verb-ing' for a repeated problem
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Kept + verb-ing' trong quá khứ mô tả một hành động xảy ra lặp đi lặp lại mà bạn không muốn. Ví dụ: 'the sauce kept going too thin' nghĩa là nước sốt cứ bị loãng mãi, không phải chỉ một lần.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Kept + verb-ing' in the past describes an action that happened repeatedly despite your efforts. Example: 'the sauce kept going too thin' means it became too thin again and again, not just once.

sampleTranslationVi: Hai năm trước, tôi quyết định thử làm moussaka lần đầu tiên. Tôi đã ăn nó một lần tại một nhà hàng Hy Lạp nhỏ ở thành phố của mình và muốn làm tại nhà. Tôi tìm thấy công thức online, nhưng khi nhìn vào các nguyên liệu, tôi nhận ra mình không thể tìm thấy một trong số đó ở siêu thị gần nhà. Công thức sử dụng một loại phô mai cụ thể mà không ai nghe nói đến ở đất nước tôi, vì vậy tôi mua một loại địa phương tương tự thay thế và hy vọng nó sẽ ổn. Phần khó nhất là làm nước sốt, cần phải đặc nhưng cứ bị loãng. Tôi thêm bột mì thêm hai lần. Khi cuối cùng cho mọi thứ vào lò nướng, tôi chờ một tiếng đồng hồ. Mùi thơm ngon và tôi cảm thấy hy vọng. Khi nếm thử muỗng đầu tiên, tôi thấy nó khá khác với phiên bản ở nhà hàng. Cà tím hơi chưa chín và nước sốt nhiều bột quá. Nhưng nó cũng ấm và no bụng và tôi ăn một phần lớn. Tôi gửi ảnh cho chị gái và chị trả lời: 'Trông không giống moussaka chút nào. Đó có phải khoai tây sống không?' Tôi cười. Lần thứ hai tôi làm, ba tuần sau, nó ngon hơn nhiều.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct way to say you tried something for the first time.
options: I decided to try making moussaka for the first time.
I decided try making moussaka for the first time.
I decided to try to make moussaka for the first time.
I decided trying making moussaka for the first time.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Decided to + infinitive' and 'try + verb-ing' are both correct. 'Decided to try making' correctly chains the two verbs. 'Decided trying' is incorrect.
mcQuestions.hint: What comes after 'decided'? What comes after 'try' when you experiment with doing something?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the correct way to say something continued happening repeatedly despite your efforts.
options: The sauce kept going too thin.
The sauce kept go too thin.
The sauce kept to go too thin.
The sauce keeps going too thin.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Keep + verb-ing' describes a repeated, unwanted action. 'Kept going' is the past form. 'Kept to go' and 'kept go' are incorrect.
mcQuestions.hint: What form of the verb follows 'keep'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly describes a problem that you fixed more than once?
options: I add more flour twice.
I added more flour twice.
I added more flours twice.
I have added more flour twice.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Added' is past simple. 'Flour' is uncountable and has no plural. 'Have added' is present perfect and breaks the past narrative.
mcQuestions.hint: Is 'flour' countable or uncountable? What tense tells a completed past story?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct use of 'instead' to show a substitution.
options: I bought a similar one instead.
I bought a similar one instead of.
I bought a similar one instead that.
I bought a similar one in place.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Instead' alone, at the end of the clause, means 'as a substitute'. 'Instead of' must be followed by a noun or verb-ing.
mcQuestions.hint: When 'instead' ends the sentence, does it need 'of' after it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that correctly describes food that is not fully cooked.
options: The aubergines were undercooked.
The aubergines were undercooking.
The aubergines were undercook.
The aubergines were been undercooked.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Were undercooked' uses past simple 'be' + adjective. 'Undercooking' is a participle, not an adjective here.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'were', do you use an adjective or a verb form?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly reports that something looked different from what it should be?
options: It looks nothing like moussaka.
It looked nothing like moussaka.
It looked like nothing moussaka.
It looked nothing as moussaka.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Looked nothing like' is a fixed expression meaning 'did not look at all like'. The preposition is 'like', not 'as'.
mcQuestions.hint: What phrase means 'did not look at all like something'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the correct word for a different version of the same dish.
options: a version
a variation
an edition
a copy
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A version' is the natural word for a variant of a dish or recipe. 'Edition' is for books; 'copy' is for documents.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word describes a variant form of the same dish?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct way to say something was much better the next time.
options: The second time I made it, it was much better.
The second time I made it, it is much better.
Second time I made it, it was much better.
The second time I made it, it was more better.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Much better' is the correct comparative. 'More better' is non-standard. 'The second time' needs the definite article 'the'.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the correct way to intensify a comparative adjective?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence where 'feel hopeful' is used correctly in the past.
options: I felt hopeful.
I felt hopefully.
I was feeling hopefully.
I was feel hopeful.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Felt hopeful' uses the adjective 'hopeful' after the linking verb 'felt'. 'Hopefully' is an adverb and cannot follow a linking verb alone.
mcQuestions.hint: After a linking verb like 'felt', do you use an adjective or an adverb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence about searching for something online.
options: I found a recipe online.
I find a recipe online.
I have found a recipe online.
I founded a recipe online.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple 'found' is correct. 'Founded' means established (an organisation). 'Have found' is present perfect.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the difference between 'found' (past of 'find') and 'founded' (past of 'found' = establish)?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'realise' with a past clause?
options: I realised I could not find the ingredient.
I realised I cannot find the ingredient.
I realise I could not find the ingredient.
I realised I couldn't to find the ingredient.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Realised' + past clause ('could not find') keeps the tense consistent. 'Cannot' is present tense. 'Couldn't to find' is incorrect — modal + base verb, no 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: After a past reporting verb like 'realised', what tense goes in the clause?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever tried to recreate a dish and found that your version was different from the original? Did that difference matter to you, and why?
